content.text: The AR(p) Model
An autoregressive model of order p, written AR(p), expresses the current value as a linear combination of the previous p values plus white noise:

c is a constant (the unconditional mean times 1 − Σφᵢ), φ₁, …, φₚ are the autoregressive coefficients, and εₜ is the innovation — the unpredictable part. If all roots of the characteristic polynomial 1 − φ₁z − … − φₚzᵖ lie outside the unit circle in the complex plane, the process is stationary: it has constant mean and variance regardless of when you observe it.

import numpy as np

def simulate_ar(phi, c, sigma, T, rng=None):
    """
    phi:  array of AR coefficients (length p)
    c:    constant term
    sigma: noise std dev
    T:    number of time steps to generate
    """
    rng = rng or np.random.default_rng()
    p = len(phi)
    x = np.zeros(T)
    # Initialise with zeros (burn-in discarded in practice)
    for t in range(p, T):
        x[t] = c + phi @ x[t-p:t][::-1] + rng.normal(0, sigma)
    return x[p:]

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
# AR(2): x_t = 0.6 x_{t-1} + 0.2 x_{t-2} + ε
x = simulate_ar(phi=np.array([0.6, 0.2]), c=0.0, sigma=1.0, T=1000, rng=rng)
print(f"mean={x.mean():.3f}  std={x.std():.3f}")
# mean≈0, std≈1.3 (higher than noise because autocorrelation amplifies)
The MA(q) Model
A moving average model of order q expresses the current value as a linear combination of the current and previous q innovations:

MA models have autocorrelation that cuts off exactly at lag q — a sharp diagnostic signature. They are always stationary (any finite MA process is stationary), but not all MA processes are invertible.
ARMA(p, q): Combining Both
ARMA(p, q) combines autoregressive and moving average components:
Often a low-order ARMA(1,1) or ARMA(2,1) can represent a pattern that would require a much higher-order pure AR or MA model, leading to more parsimonious fits.
ARIMA for Non-Stationary Series

Many real-world series (prices, cumulative counts) have a trending mean — they are non-stationary. ARIMA(p, d, q) extends ARMA by differencing the series d times to remove trends. The differenced series Δᵈxₜ is then modelled as ARMA(p, q).
Model
Parameters
Use when
AR(p)
φ₁…φₚ, σ²
PACF cuts off at lag p
MA(q)
θ₁…θq, σ²
ACF cuts off at lag q
ARMA(p,q)
φ₁…φₚ, θ₁…θq, σ²
Both ACF and PACF tail off gradually
ARIMA(p,d,q)
p, d, q
Non-stationary with linear trend (d=1) or quadratic (d=2)
Fitting with statsmodels

# pip install statsmodels
import numpy as np
from statsmodels.tsa.arima.model import ARIMA

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
# Simulate AR(2) series
phi_true = np.array([0.7, -0.2])
T = 300
x = np.zeros(T)
for t in range(2, T):
    x[t] = phi_true @ x[t-2:t][::-1] + rng.normal(0, 1)

# Fit ARIMA(2,0,0) = AR(2)
result = ARIMA(x, order=(2, 0, 0)).fit()
print(result.summary().tables[1])  # coefficient table
print(f"AR1={result.arparams[0]:.3f}  AR2={result.arparams[1]:.3f}")
# AR1≈0.70  AR2≈-0.20

# Forecast next 5 steps
forecast = result.forecast(steps=5)
print("Forecast:", forecast.round(2))
Order Selection
Choosing p and q is a model selection problem. Two standard criteria penalise log-likelihood by model complexity:

where k is the number of free parameters and T is the sample size. BIC penalises complexity more heavily and tends to select sparser models. In practice, fit a grid of (p, q) values and pick the order with the lowest AIC or BIC:

import warnings
import itertools

best_aic = np.inf
best_order = None
for p, q in itertools.product(range(5), range(4)):
    try:
        with warnings.catch_warnings():
            warnings.simplefilter("ignore")
            res = ARIMA(x, order=(p, 0, q)).fit()
        if res.aic < best_aic:
            best_aic   = res.aic
            best_order = (p, 0, q)
    except Exception:
        pass
print(f"Best order by AIC: ARIMA{best_order}  AIC={best_aic:.2f}")
# Best order by AIC: ARIMA(2, 0, 0)  AIC=...
Generative Perspective

ARMA models are probabilistic generative models: once the parameters (φ, θ, σ) are estimated, you can sample new sequences by iterating the defining equation forward in time with fresh innovations drawn from N(0, σ²). This is the same autoregressive generation principle used by neural language models — predict the next token (or value), sample from the predicted distribution, append to context, repeat.

The main difference is that ARMA parametrises the conditional distribution as a linear function of past values, while neural models use deep non-linear functions. Linear models are interpretable and fit in milliseconds on small datasets; neural models scale to high-dimensional, complex time series at the cost of compute and interpretability.
